step.title: Step 25
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP

sections.content:
The code executed 16 actions in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 71, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Plan: Navigate cursor back to top grid area and try clicking crosses from there
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 55 of about 81 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The cursor is now at row [33,36], col [43,46]. This is on the right side of the horizontal path. The UP command didn't move it, so the cursor can't go UP from here either.
Looking at the image, the cursor is now on the right side of the horizontal path, below the top grid. The top grid has crosses at (16, 38) and (16, 44).
The cursor is at col [43,46], which aligns with the cross at (16, 44). But the cursor can't move UP into the top grid.
Let me reconsider the mechanic. Maybe the cursor doesn't need to enter the grid. Maybe the mechanic is:
1. Position the cursor at a specific location on the path
2. Click a cross to move it to the cursor's position
Or maybe:
1. Click a cross to select it
2. Move the cursor to the destination
3. Use ACTION7 to place it
But clicking crosses doesn't seem to select them (no G border appears).
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe I need to click the cross at (16, 44) while the cursor is at col [43,46] to see if that triggers something.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the level 1 mechanic. In level 1, I clicked crosses and they moved to the right. The g pattern appeared at the destination. But in level 2, clicking crosses doesn't produce any effect.
Maybe the mechanic in level 2 is that I need to use the cursor to "push" or "pull" crosses. Let me try clicking the cross at (16, 44) while the cursor is nearby.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Maybe the cursor needs to be at a specific "target" cell in the grid, and then I click a cross to move it there. But the cursor can't enter the grid...
Wait, let me look at the path structure more carefully. The path connects to the top grid on the right side. Maybe I need to move the cursor UP from the right side to enter the top grid.
Let me try moving the cursor to the far right and then UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- Cursor at row [33,36], col [43,46] - right side of horizontal path
- Can't move UP into top grid from here
- Need to find a way to interact with crosses
Plan: Try clicking the cross at (16, 44) while cursor is nearby, or try moving cursor further right and UP

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
from collections import Counter

# Try clicking the cross at (16, 44) while cursor is at col [43,46]
print("=== Click cross at (16, 44) ===")
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 44}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
N_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
g_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'g']
G_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G']
print(f"After click: N={len(N_nodes)}, g={len(g_nodes)}, G={len(G_nodes)}")

# Check if anything changed
if len(N_nodes) < 3:
    print("Cross moved!")
    for n in N_nodes:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
else:
    print("No change - cross didn't move")

# Try moving cursor further RIGHT and then UP
print("\n=== Move RIGHT ===")
result = action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
for n in O_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"After RIGHT: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")

# Try UP
print("\n=== Try UP ===")
result = action([{'action': 'UP'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
O_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O']
for n in O_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"After UP: row=[{min(rows)},{max(rows)}], col=[{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
